pain_points.signal: Complex regulatory/logical requirements that are inherently difficult to slice into small, independent increments.
Organizational rigidity: teams not allowed to autonomously change sprint length or processes, enforced top-down synchronization.
End-user stakeholders refusing to provide feedback on partial/incomplete functionality, demanding 100% completion.
Feeling like an 'idiot' when presenting incremental, non-user-facing progress (like a search that returns no results) to stakeholders.
Constant context churn and disruption from high-priority bugs/escalations, even within sprint structures.
Stigma or organizational barriers against multiple developers swarming a single ticket to parallelize large stories.
Pressure to deliver perfect, complete functionality without opportunities for iterative learning or scope adjustment.
Frequent reprioritization or total direction changes leading to merged but unreleased code becoming 'dead weight'.
Sprint constraints feel like the 'boss', limiting team empowerment and flexibility.

desired_outcomes.outcome: Ability to slice large, complex stories into smaller, integrable increments—even if early slices don't deliver direct user value—to enable learning, feedback, and pivotability.
Clear plate/clean slate at sprint boundaries, allowing teams to focus and PO to reprioritize without leaving work in progress.
Team autonomy to adjust processes (e.g., sprint length, swarming) based on their specific context and constraints.
Stakeholder and user willingness to engage with and provide feedback on partial/minimal functionality to guide development.
Robust CI/CD and testing setup to support working in very small increments (minutes) for rapid integration and low-context-switch pivots.
A shared understanding that 'value' includes internal learning, risk mitigation, and planning insights, not just user-facing impact.
Protection from constant disruption during sprints, allowing focused, deep work.

objections.objection: Merging incomplete, non-value-delivering increments creates 'dead weight' code that may never be used.
Vertical slicing (e.g., building a search with no results) produces increments users don't want and is not a valid 'user story'.
Sprint constraints can feel disempowering, making the sprint the 'boss' rather than enabling team self-management.
If stakeholders expect polished, complete features, presenting thin slices can make the team look incompetent.
Inherent complexity (e.g., financial regulations) makes true vertical/value-based slicing practically impossible.
If priorities shift too rapidly, the effort spent slicing and integrating is wasted.

decision_factors_ranked: Ability to enable iterative learning and feedback loops
Support for team autonomy and process flexibility
Facilitation of clean work boundaries and focus
Tooling for CI/CD and fast integration
deal_breakers: Organizational mandates that prevent team-level process adaptation (e.g., fixed sprint lengths for all teams)
Stakeholder culture that refuses to engage with anything less than 100% complete functionality
Inability to break down complex work into integrable increments, leading to multi-sprint WIP and feedback black holes

pain_points.signal: Slicing user stories into non-valuable chunks just to fit a sprint
